planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination after natural cold stratification, or start indoors 8 weeks before last frost; elecampane is easy from seed but takes 2 years from seed before the root is large enough to harvest for medicine. Roots can be divided in fall or spring to propagate more quickly.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; elecampane is one of the most majestic of all European medicinal herbs, forming towering clumps with leaves up to 2 feet long at the base and smaller clasping leaves up the stem, topped in summer with large, shaggy yellow composite flowers resembling coarse sunflowers. The enormous root, harvested in fall of the second or third year, has been used in European and Asian herbal medicine for respiratory conditions and as an antibacterial agent since antiquity — modern research confirms that the root contains the compound alantolactone with antimicrobial properties. A native of central Asia and southeastern Europe, now naturalized in moist meadows and roadsides across eastern North America. Excellent background plant for the herb or medicinal garden, providing bold architectural structure.

planting.note: Propagate from stem cuttings or root division in spring; horse mint spreads vigorously by underground runners like all mints and is best contained in a pot or a bed with root barriers. Plant in moist soil in partial shade.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade, contained in pots or with root barriers to limit spreading; horse mint is a European native mint with long, lance-shaped, silver-green leaves that are more strongly and sharply aromatic than spearmint, with a mentholated, camphor-like fragrance. The dense, elongated spikes of small lavender flowers in late summer are exceptionally attractive to bumblebees, honey bees, and numerous other pollinators. The leaves can be used as a tea, culinary seasoning, or insect repellent — the intense menthol content makes it more suited to teas and infusions than to fresh eating. In South Africa it is widely used as a traditional medicine and culinary herb (called 'ballerja'); it naturalizes in moist streamside habitats across North Africa, Europe, and western Asia. Contrast with 'horse mint' used in the US (Monarda punctata), which is an entirely unrelated native wildflower.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; or cold-stratify seeds at 40°F for 4–6 weeks, then sow indoors 8 weeks before last frost. Southern beardtongue tolerates poor, sandy, acidic soil better than most penstemons.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy or rocky, lean, acidic soil in full sun; southern beardtongue is a slender, wiry native of the southeastern Coastal Plain from Virginia to Florida, well adapted to the acidic sandhills, pine flatwoods, and open dry woods where most penstemons would fail. The tubular flowers in late spring through early summer are pink-lavender with two-lipped openings, arranged on spikes that attract ruby-throated hummingbirds and bumblebees. Unlike many penstemons, this species tolerates the humid heat of zones 7–9 and the highly acidic, low-fertility sandy soils characteristic of the southeastern Coastal Plain. Short-lived as a perennial (typically 3–5 years) but self-seeds reliably to maintain a colony. An underutilized native wildflower for naturalistic plantings in the Southeast.

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface in spring after last frost — seeds need light to germinate; in zone 9, sow in early spring (February–March) or in late summer for fall flowers. The plant behaves as an annual in most of the South but sometimes overwinters in zones 8–10.

directions: Scatter seeds on bare, dry, well-drained soil in full sun after last frost — this wildflower requires excellent drainage and poor, even sandy soil; in rich, moist soil it becomes floppy and short-lived. Lanceleaf blanket flower is native to sandy plains and open disturbed ground from Virginia to Florida and west to Texas, with narrow lance-shaped leaves (unlike the wider leaves of the hybrid garden gaillardia) and bright yellow to orange-tipped ray flowers with purple-red centers that bloom continuously from summer through frost. A superb plant for hot, dry sandy gardens and roadsides in the Southeast where it attracts skippers, bumblebees, and goldfinches (which eat the seed heads). Minimal care once established — simply leave the seed heads in place for self-sowing.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or plant divisions in spring or fall in well-drained, average to poor soil; willowleaf sunflower spreads by underground rhizomes and can form large colonies — give it space to spread in naturalized prairie or meadow plantings.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to poor, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; willowleaf sunflower is a striking tall prairie native from the Great Plains and limestone hills of the Midwest, instantly recognizable by its unusually narrow, grass-like leaves that cascade down the tall stems — quite unlike the broad leaves of common sunflowers. The plant blooms later than most sunflowers, with masses of small golden-yellow flowers from August through October, providing a critical late-season nectar and pollen source for migrating monarchs, native bees, and skippers. Extremely drought tolerant once established. The seeds are eaten by goldfinches and sparrows through winter. Excellent at the back of a prairie planting, in a meadow garden, or as a dramatic vertical accent. The cultivar 'First Light' stays at 4 feet; the species at full size can reach 6–8 feet.
